content.text: What PI Planning Is and Why GenAI Needs Adaptations

Program Increment (PI) Planning is a SAFe cadence event — typically 8–12 weeks — where all Agile Release Train (ART) teams align on objectives, resolve cross-team dependencies, and commit to a PI plan. GenAI initiatives introduce constraints that standard PI Planning does not anticipate: model deprecation mid-PI, compute budget caps, non-deterministic evaluation cycles, and regulatory review gates that may block feature releases independently of engineering readiness.
PI Planning does not change its purpose for GenAI — it remains the ART's synchronisation heartbeat. But the inputs, risk categories, and commitment vocabulary all need explicit GenAI extensions.
PI Planning Event Structure — GenAI Adaptations

attrs.alt: PI Planning for GenAI — 2-Day Event Structure

content.text: The table above maps each PI Planning slot to the GenAI-specific adaptation required. Three slots are new or significantly modified:
Architecture & GenAI Constraints briefing

— a dedicated slot before team breakouts where the platform architect presents current model availability, latency/cost budgets, GPU quota allocations, the approved eval framework, and any active data-governance restrictions. Teams cannot commit without this context.
Confidence vote semantics
— the standard fist-of-five vote is extended so a score below 3 on any team triggers immediate scope renegotiation, not just a flag. Uncertainty about model quality is a valid reason to reduce scope.
Compute budget allocation
— a new closing slot where Finance and the platform team allocate GPU-hours and API-token budgets per ART for the PI, with explicit overage-escalation thresholds.
Writing PI Objectives for GenAI Features

Standard PI objectives state a business outcome and a stretch goal. GenAI objectives add two fields: an evaluation metric commitment and a confidence percentage derived from current baseline performance.

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import Optional


@dataclass
class PIObjective:
    team: str
    description: str
    business_value: int          # 1–10, scored by Product Management
    stretch: bool = False

    # GenAI extensions
    eval_metric: Optional[str] = None   # e.g. "ROUGE-L ≥ 0.42 on held-out set"
    confidence_pct: Optional[int] = None  # 0–100; below 60 flags as stretch auto
    compute_units_gpu_h: float = 0.0     # GPU-hours budgeted for this objective

    def __post_init__(self):
        if self.confidence_pct is not None and self.confidence_pct < 60:
            self.stretch = True   # low-confidence objectives are always stretch

    def summary(self) -> str:
        flag = "[STRETCH] " if self.stretch else ""
        conf = f" (confidence {self.confidence_pct}%)" if self.confidence_pct is not None else ""
        metric = f"\n  Eval: {self.eval_metric}" if self.eval_metric else ""
        return f"{flag}{self.description}{conf}{metric}"


# Example PI objectives for a document-summarisation ART
objectives = [
    PIObjective(
        team="Summarisation Team",
        description="Ship v1 extractive summariser with latency < 3 s p95",
        business_value=8,
        eval_metric="ROUGE-L ≥ 0.40 on 500-doc evaluation set",
        confidence_pct=80,
        compute_units_gpu_h=120.0,
    ),
    PIObjective(
        team="Summarisation Team",
        description="Prototype abstractive model on internal corpus",
        business_value=5,
        eval_metric="BERTScore F1 ≥ 0.72",
        confidence_pct=45,     # auto-flagged stretch
        compute_units_gpu_h=400.0,
    ),
]

for obj in objectives:
    print(obj.summary())
The
confidence_pct

field makes uncertainty explicit at planning time rather than surfacing as a sprint-level surprise. Product Management can decide in the room whether to promote a 45%-confidence item to stretch or drop it from the PI entirely.
Managing Dependencies Across GenAI and Non-GenAI Teams
In a mixed ART — some teams building GenAI features, others building conventional software that consumes them — the dependency board needs a new category:
model contract dependencies

from dataclasses import dataclass
from enum import Enum


class DependencyType(Enum):
    FEATURE = "feature"           # team A needs an API from team B
    DATA = "data"                 # team A needs a labelled dataset from team B
    MODEL_CONTRACT = "model_contract"  # team A needs a stable model interface/version
    COMPUTE = "compute"           # team A needs GPU quota approved by platform


@dataclass
class Dependency:
    from_team: str
    to_team: str
    dep_type: DependencyType
    description: str
    needed_by_sprint: int         # sprint within the PI (1-indexed)
    # For model contracts: which model version and endpoint is being relied upon
    model_version: str = ""
    endpoint: str = ""

    def is_risky(self) -> bool:
        """Flag as risky if model contract depends on an external provider."""
        return (
            self.dep_type == DependencyType.MODEL_CONTRACT
            and "gpt" in self.model_version.lower()
        )

    def roam_label(self) -> str:
        """Suggest initial ROAM categorisation for the risk board."""
        if self.is_risky():
            return "MITIGATE"     # external model deprecation — need fallback plan
        if self.dep_type == DependencyType.COMPUTE:
            return "OWN"          # internal resource, can be resolved
        return "ACCEPT"


# ── PI planning dependency board example ──
deps = [
    Dependency(
        from_team="Chat UI Team",
        to_team="LLM Gateway Team",
        dep_type=DependencyType.MODEL_CONTRACT,
        description="Stable /v1/chat endpoint with streaming support",
        needed_by_sprint=2,
        model_version="gpt-4o-2024-08-06",
        endpoint="/v1/chat/completions",
    ),
    Dependency(
        from_team="Summarisation Team",
        to_team="Data Engineering",
        dep_type=DependencyType.DATA,
        description="2 000-document labelled summarisation corpus",
        needed_by_sprint=1,
    ),
]

for d in deps:
    print(f"{d.from_team} ← {d.to_team} [{d.dep_type.value}] → ROAM: {d.roam_label()}")

Model contract dependencies are particularly susceptible to external provider deprecation. Raising them on the ROAM board during PI Planning — rather than discovering them mid-sprint — gives the ART time to negotiate stable version pinning or build a local fallback.
The GenAI ROAM Risk Board
ROAM categorises each risk as Resolved, Owned, Accepted, or Mitigated. GenAI programs surface several risk classes that rarely appear in conventional ARTs:
Risk Class
Typical Source
ROAM Action
Owner
Model deprecation
Provider end-of-life notice
MITIGATE — pin version + build fallback
Platform Architect
Data drift
Production distribution shift
OWN — add drift detector to PI scope
ML Engineering
Eval metric ambiguity
Disagreement on quality threshold
RESOLVE — agree metric before sprint 1
Product Manager
Regulatory review delay
AI Act / internal ethics board
ACCEPT or MITIGATE — stage release
Programme Manager
GPU quota shortfall
Cloud provider cap / cost overrun
OWN — platform requests increase or reduces scope
Platform / Finance
Prompt injection vector
New attack surface discovered
MITIGATE — add red-team sprint to PI
Security
The ROAM board for a GenAI ART should be reviewed in every Scrum-of-Scrums during the PI, not only at PI Planning, because model and data risks can materialise faster than a quarterly cadence.
Compute Budget Allocation During PI Planning

Compute budget allocation is the GenAI-specific closing ceremony of PI Planning. The platform team arrives with a total GPU-hour and API-token envelope for the PI; teams declare their budgets from objectives; Finance ratifies the allocation.

Leaving at least 10–15% of the GPU budget unallocated acts as a buffer for unplanned experiments, hotfixes, and evaluation reruns. Teams that exhaust their allocation before sprint 4 of a 5-sprint PI typically slow down disproportionately because they enter a queue for shared capacity.
Cadence: PI Length and Inspect-and-Adapt Ceremonies
Standard SAFe recommends a 10-week PI with five 2-week sprints. GenAI programs often find that 8 weeks fits better because:
Model capability improvements arrive faster than quarterly — a shorter PI means teams can respond to new model versions without waiting a full cycle.
Evaluation cycles for large models can take an entire sprint. An 8-week PI with a dedicated evaluation sprint in the middle aligns planning to this reality.
Regulatory review timelines in AI are unpredictable — shorter PIs reduce the cost of a forced slip.
The Inspect and Adapt (I&A) workshop at the end of each PI should include a dedicated GenAI retrospective slot covering:
Were our eval metric commitments met? What caused gaps?
Which model or data dependencies caused the most friction?
Did our compute budget allocation match actual consumption? Where was the error?
What ROAM risks materialised, and how well did our mitigations work?
Integrating Non-SAFe Teams Into PI Planning
Not every GenAI program runs on SAFe. When a GenAI initiative sits inside a LeSS or Scrum@Scale organisation, PI Planning maps as follows:
PI Planning Element
LeSS Equivalent
Scrum@Scale Equivalent
PI Objectives
Sprint Goals aggregated to an Overall Sprint Goal
Team Sprint Goals fed into MetaScrum Product Backlog priority
Dependency board
Multi-team Sprint Planning coordination board
Scrum-of-Scrums dependency table
ROAM risk board
Overall Retrospective risk log
Executive MetaScrum escalation list
Compute budget allocation
LeSS Overall Sprint Planning resource allocation
MetaScrum quarterly budget review
Confidence vote
Team capacity check in Sprint Planning Part 1
SoS team-confidence check

The underlying practice — explicit objective commitment, dependency surfacing, and resource allocation — translates across all three frameworks. The ceremony name and cadence differ; the information exchanged does not.
Anti-Patterns in GenAI PI Planning
Committing to accuracy targets without baseline data
— teams cannot commit to "improve BLEU score by 5 points" without a measured baseline. Require baselines to be established before PI Planning, not during it.
Treating model upgrades as zero-cost
— switching from one model to another always requires regression testing on the eval suite, prompt re-tuning, and often integration retesting. Budget for this explicitly.
No model contract on the dependency board
— if the UI team depends on a specific model endpoint version that the Gateway team hasn't pinned, the dependency is invisible until it breaks.
Over-allocating compute to the most vocal team
— compute allocation should be proportional to PI objectives, not negotiating strength. Use the
ComputeBudget.validate()
pattern to make the math visible.
Skipping the architecture briefing to save time
— teams that commit without knowing the GPU quota or model availability frequently discover blockers in sprint 1 that require de-scoping mid-PI.
Pre-PI Planning: Preparation Activities Specific to GenAI
The quality of a PI Planning event depends heavily on preparation done in the week before. For GenAI programs, three additional preparation activities are essential:
1. Model Readiness Assessment

The platform architect produces a model readiness report covering: current model versions and their deprecation timelines, benchmark performance on the ART's evaluation sets, known limitations (context-window constraints, hallucination rates on domain-specific prompts), and any pending fine-tuning or RAG index updates that will land during the upcoming PI.

from dataclasses import dataclass
from datetime import date
from typing import Optional


@dataclass
class ModelReadiness:
    model_id: str
    version: str
    provider: str
    deprecation_date: Optional[date]
    latency_p95_ms: float
    eval_score: float            # domain-specific benchmark, 0–1
    context_window_tokens: int
    fine_tuned: bool
    known_issues: list[str]

    def is_safe_for_pi(self, pi_end_date: date) -> bool:
        """Model is only safe if it won't be deprecated mid-PI."""
        if self.deprecation_date is None:
            return True
        return self.deprecation_date > pi_end_date

    def readiness_summary(self, pi_end_date: date) -> str:
        safe = "SAFE" if self.is_safe_for_pi(pi_end_date) else "RISK — deprecated before PI ends"
        return (
            f"{self.model_id} v{self.version} | eval={self.eval_score:.2f} | "
            f"p95={self.latency_p95_ms:.0f}ms | {safe}"
        )


# Pre-PI readiness checks
models = [
    ModelReadiness(
        model_id="gpt-4o",
        version="2024-08-06",
        provider="openai",
        deprecation_date=date(2026, 9, 1),
        latency_p95_ms=1800,
        eval_score=0.84,
        context_window_tokens=128000,
        fine_tuned=False,
        known_issues=["JSON mode unstable on >8k prompt"],
    ),
    ModelReadiness(
        model_id="llama-3-70b",
        version="instruct-q4",
        provider="self-hosted",
        deprecation_date=None,
        latency_p95_ms=900,
        eval_score=0.76,
        context_window_tokens=8192,
        fine_tuned=True,
        known_issues=[],
    ),
]

pi_end = date(2026, 9, 15)
for m in models:
    print(m.readiness_summary(pi_end))
2. Evaluation Suite Freeze

Changing the evaluation suite mid-PI invalidates the baseline that PI objectives were written against. The week before PI Planning, the team responsible for evaluation infrastructure must freeze the eval suite: no new test cases, no metric changes, no dataset updates until the PI ends. Changes requested during the PI are queued for the next cycle.
Documenting this freeze formally prevents the common failure mode where teams "improve" their eval metric by relaxing the test set rather than improving the model.
3. Dependency Pre-Negotiation

Model contract dependencies are the most likely to surprise teams during PI Planning. The RTE should run a brief pre-PI sync with team leads one week before the event to surface any known model contract risks. Teams arrive at PI Planning with a draft dependency list rather than discovering dependencies in the room, which reduces the event's scope-negotiation overhead.
Running the Confidence Vote for GenAI Objectives

The SAFe confidence vote — a fist-of-five show of hands — is an informal signal in standard ARTs. For GenAI programs it needs to be structured more precisely because low confidence often reflects genuine model uncertainty rather than planning incompleteness.
When the vote returns
RENEGOTIATE
, the facilitator initiates an immediate scope discussion. The typical resolution for GenAI objectives is one of:
Move the objective to stretch (retaining the intent but removing the commitment).
Reduce scope — for example, lowering the eval threshold from 0.42 to 0.38 ROUGE-L.
Split the objective: commit to the retrieval layer, make the generative layer a stretch.
Remove the objective from the PI and add it to the next PI Planning input.

Leaving a RENEGOTIATE objective in the committed list without resolution is the most common failure mode in GenAI PI Planning. It creates a false sense of commitment that collapses at the first sprint review.
Summary
PI Planning for GenAI programs follows the same two-day structure as standard SAFe but adds five concrete adaptations:
An Architecture & GenAI Constraints briefing before team breakouts.
PI Objectives extended with evaluation metric commitments and confidence percentages.
A GenAI-specific ROAM category set covering model deprecation, data drift, eval ambiguity, regulatory delay, compute shortfall, and security risks.
A closing Compute Budget Allocation ceremony with explicit team-level GPU and token budgets.
A shorter PI cadence (8 weeks recommended) with an I&A slot dedicated to eval and compute retrospectives.

These adaptations make the non-determinism and infrastructure constraints of GenAI work visible at the ART level, preventing the late-sprint surprises that occur when teams treat model quality as a soft assumption rather than a hard commitment with a measurable threshold.
